cards.title: General Inspection

pearls.label: 🟡 Clinical Pearl
pearls.text: Inspection before palpation — always. The end-of-bed assessment gives you the gestalt of how sick the patient is. A patient who looks well despite abnormal observations is different from one who looks unwell with normal observations.

steps.title: Position & environment
steps.text: — Patient at 45°, chest exposed, adequate lighting. Look from the end of the bed before touching.

steps.title: Face & peripheries
steps.text: — Malar flush (mitral stenosis), pallor, central cyanosis (lips/tongue), jaundice (right heart failure), corneal arcus, xanthelasma

steps.title: Hands
steps.text: — Clubbing, koilonychia, splinter haemorrhages (IE), Osler's nodes (painful, pulp, IE), Janeway lesions (painless, palms, IE), pallor (anaemia)

steps.title: Chest
steps.text: — Sternotomy scar (CABG/valve replacement), thoracotomy scar, pacemaker/ICD device, chest deformity (pectus excavatum → MVP)

steps.title: Legs
steps.text: — Pitting oedema (bilateral = cardiac/hypoalbuminaemia, unilateral = DVT/venous), varicosities, peripheral cyanosis

cards.title: Pulse Assessment

alerts.label: Radio-radial & radio-femoral delay
alerts.text: Simultaneous palpation: delay between right and left radial = subclavian artery stenosis or aortic coarctation. Radio-femoral delay = coarctation of aorta.

rows: Rapid rise and fall — best felt with arm raised
CO₂ retention, sepsis, thyrotoxicosis, anaemia, pregnancy

pearls.label: 🟡 Pearl
pearls.text: Always check the pulse for a full 30 seconds in suspected AF — feel for the irregularity. A regularly irregular pulse (missed beats) = ectopics or Wenckebach. An irregularly irregular pulse = AF until proven otherwise.

cards.title: Blood Pressure

steps.title: Correct technique
steps.text: — Patient seated or supine, 5 minutes rest, arm supported at heart level, correct cuff size (bladder should encircle 80% of arm circumference)

steps.title: Estimate systolic first
steps.text: — Inflate until radial pulse disappears, note pressure, deflate, re-inflate 20–30 mmHg above this estimate

steps.title: Deflate slowly
steps.text: — 2–3 mmHg/sec. Korotkoff Phase I (first sound) = systolic. Phase V (disappearance) = diastolic

steps.title: Both arms
steps.text: — >20 mmHg difference = subclavian stenosis or aortic dissection (use the higher reading)

steps.title: Lying and standing
steps.text: — Postural hypotension = >20 mmHg SBP drop or >10 mmHg DBP drop after 3 minutes standing → autonomic failure, dehydration, medications

cards.title: JVP Assessment

alerts.label: Technique
alerts.text: Patient at 45°, head rotated slightly left. Look for the double waveform (a-c-x-v-y) in the right internal jugular vein. Do NOT confuse with carotid pulse (JVP is non-pulsatile with light pressure, has double waveform, rises on abdominal pressure).

rows: Right heart failure ★, tamponade, constrictive pericarditis, SVC obstruction, fluid overload
Atrial contraction against closed tricuspid valve → CHB, VT, junctional rhythm
JVP RISES on inspiration → constrictive pericarditis, RV infarction, cardiac tamponade
Firm RUQ pressure → sustained JVP rise >3cm → right heart failure

pearls.label: 🟡 Pearl
pearls.text: The JVP is a window into the right atrium. A raised JVP is one of the earliest signs of right heart failure. In cardiac tamponade, remember Beck's triad: raised JVP + hypotension + muffled heart sounds. Pulsus paradoxus also present.

cards.title: Precordial Examination

pearls.label: 🟡 Pearl
pearls.text: Always use the flat of the hand for thrills (more sensitive than fingertips). A thrill means the murmur is at least grade 4/6. The absence of a thrill does NOT mean the murmur is not significant.

steps.title: Apex beat — location
steps.text: — Normally 5th ICS, midclavicular line. Displaced laterally/inferiorly = cardiomegaly (LV dilatation). Non-palpable = COPD, obesity, pericardial effusion, dextrocardia

steps.title: Apex beat — character
steps.text: — Heaving (pressure overload: AS, hypertension) / Thrusting (volume overload: AR, MR) / Tapping (palpable S1: MS) / Dyskinetic (LV aneurysm)

steps.title: Parasternal heave
steps.text: — Left hand flat on sternum: sustained outward lift = right ventricular hypertrophy (cor pulmonale, pulmonary hypertension)

steps.title: Thrills
steps.text: — Palpable murmur (grade ≥4). Systolic thrill at base = AS. At apex = MR. At LLSE = VSD. Continuous = PDA.

cards.title: Cardiac Auscultation

alerts.label: Auscultation areas
alerts.text: Aortic: 2nd right ICS · Pulmonary: 2nd left ICS · Tricuspid: lower left sternal edge · Mitral: apex (5th ICS midclavicular line)

rows: Mitral + tricuspid closure. Loud in MS, soft in MR. Split S1 = RBBB or Ebstein's
Aortic + pulmonary closure. Fixed split = ASD. Paradoxical split = LBBB, AS. Loud P2 = pulmonary hypertension
Normal in young/pregnancy. Pathological in adults = heart failure (ventricular dilatation), MR, VSD
Always abnormal. Stiff ventricle: LVH, hypertension, HOCM, AS, ischaemia
Mitral stenosis. Closer snap to S2 = more severe MS
Mitral valve prolapse (may be followed by a late systolic murmur)
Pericarditis — scratchy, leathery. Best heard end-expiration with patient leaning forward.

pearls.label: 🟡 Pearl
pearls.text: Auscultate in multiple positions: sitting forward (aortic regurgitation murmur louder), left lateral decubitus (mitral murmurs louder), standing/squatting (HOCM: murmur louder on standing, softer on squatting). Always listen at the carotids for AR radiation and in the axilla for MR.

alerts.label: Grading murmurs (Levine scale)
alerts.text: 1 = barely audible · 2 = soft but easily heard · 3 = moderately loud · 4 = loud + palpable thrill · 5 = very loud, heard with stethoscope partially off chest · 6 = heard without stethoscope

Louder on standing/Valsalva, softer on squatting/hand grip. Jerky pulse.

cards.title: Respiratory Inspection

steps.title: General
steps.text: — Patient at 45°, chest exposed. Count RR for 30 seconds (normal 12–20/min). Note work of breathing: accessory muscle use, intercostal recession, nasal flaring, pursed lip breathing

steps.title: Breathing pattern
steps.text: — Kussmaul (deep, sighing = metabolic acidosis), Cheyne-Stokes (waxing/waning + apnoea = bilateral cortical lesion, CCF), Biot's (irregular with apnoea = brainstem lesion)

steps.title: Hands
steps.text: — Clubbing (fibrosis, lung ca, bronchiectasis, empyema), peripheral cyanosis, nicotine staining, CO₂ retention flap (asterixis — outstretched hands)

steps.title: Face
steps.text: — Central cyanosis (tongue/lips — SpO₂ <85%), Horner's syndrome (ptosis, miosis, anhidrosis — Pancoast tumour), plethoric face (polycythaemia, SVC obstruction)

steps.title: Chest shape
steps.text: — Barrel chest (COPD — AP diameter increased), pectus excavatum/carinatum, Harrison's sulcus (childhood respiratory disease), kyphoscoliosis (restrictive lung disease)

steps.title: Trachea
steps.text: — Central? Deviation toward collapse/fibrosis. Away from tension pneumothorax/large effusion. Note use of SCM (accessory muscle) = respiratory distress.

cards.title: Chest Expansion & Percussion

rows: Consolidation ★, collapse, pleural effusion (stony dull), haemothorax

pearls.label: 🟡 Pearl
pearls.text: In pleural effusion: stony dull percussion + decreased breath sounds + decreased vocal resonance + decreased expansion on that side. In consolidation: dull percussion + bronchial breathing + increased vocal resonance/tactile fremitus + aegophony. The combination tells the story.

steps.title: Expansion
steps.text: — Hands around lower chest, thumbs meet at midline. Normal expansion = 5+ cm. Reduced unilaterally = pleural effusion, pneumonia, collapse, pneumothorax on that side

steps.title: Tactile vocal fremitus
steps.text: — Ulnar edge of hand. "99". Increased = consolidation. Reduced = effusion, pneumothorax, collapse

steps.title: Percussion technique
steps.text: — Middle finger of non-dominant hand flat on chest (hyperextended). Strike with tip of dominant middle finger. Compare sides systematically.

cards.title: Auscultation — Breath Sounds

alerts.label: Technique
alerts.text: Ask patient to breathe through mouth. Compare side-to-side. Start at apex, work systematically to bases. Note quality, added sounds, and vocal resonance.

rows: Loud, hollow, expiration = inspiration, pause between
Consolidation ★, collapse, lung abscess, fibrous tissue
Pleural effusion, pneumothorax, collapse, COPD, obesity
Pulmonary oedema ★, pneumonia, bronchiectasis, early CCF
Upper airway obstruction: epiglottitis, croup, tumour, foreign body

pearls.label: 🟡 Pearl
pearls.text: Vocal resonance: ask patient to say "ninety-nine" while auscultating. Increased = consolidation (sound conducted through solid tissue). Decreased = effusion/pneumothorax (fluid/air blocks transmission). Whispering pectoriloquy (whispered sounds heard clearly) = consolidation. Aegophony (E→A sound change) = effusion.

cards._legacyHtml:

cards.title: Abdominal Inspection

steps.title: Position
steps.text: — Patient supine, pillow under head, arms by sides, abdomen exposed from xiphisternum to groin. Ask about pain before palpating.

steps.title: Contour
steps.text: — Distension? (5 Fs: Fat, Fluid, Flatus, Foetus, Faeces). Scaphoid (sunken) = cachexia. Visible peristalsis = bowel obstruction.

steps.title: Scars
steps.text: — Midline (laparotomy/bowel surgery), RIF (appendicectomy), RUQ (cholecystectomy), LIF (stoma reversal), Kocher's (open cholecystectomy), Rooftop (whipple/liver transplant), Mercedes-Benz (liver transplant)

steps.title: Other signs
steps.text: — Caput medusae (dilated periumbilical veins = portal hypertension), Sister Mary Joseph nodule (periumbilical node = intra-abdominal malignancy), Cullen's sign (periumbilical bruising = haemoperitoneum), Grey Turner's sign (flank bruising = retroperitoneal haemorrhage)

steps.title: Hands/peripheral signs
steps.text: — Clubbing, leukonychia, dupuytren's, palmar erythema, liver flap (asterixis), spider naevi (≥5 on trunk = portal hypertension), gynaecomastia, testicular atrophy

cards.title: Abdominal Palpation

alerts.label: Always ask about pain before palpating — start away from the painful area

pearls.label: 🟡 Pearl
pearls.text: Splenomegaly causes: mnemonic CHINA — Chronic liver disease (portal HTN), Haematological (lymphoma/leukaemia/haemolytic anaemia), Infection (EBV, malaria, TB, IE), Neoplasm, Autoimmune (SLE, RA-Felty's). Spleen must be 2–3× normal size before palpable.

steps.title: Light palpation (9 regions)
steps.text: — Superficial tenderness, guarding (voluntary/involuntary), rebound tenderness (peritoneal irritation — press then release), rigidity (board-like = peritonitis)

steps.title: Deep palpation
steps.text: — Masses: site, size, shape, surface (smooth/irregular), consistency, mobility, pulsatility, movement with respiration. Note tenderness with deep palpation.

steps.title: Liver
steps.text: — Start at RIF, move up with expiration. Normal upper border at 5th ICS (percussion). Measure in cm below costal margin (normal <2cm). Character: smooth (hepatitis/CCF), nodular (cirrhosis/metastases), tender (hepatitis/CCF), pulsatile (TR).

steps.title: Spleen
steps.text: — Start at RIF, palpate diagonally toward left costal margin. Cannot get above it. Has a notch on medial border. Ask patient to take deep breath. If not palpable: roll patient to right lateral and try again.

steps.title: Kidneys
steps.text: — Bimanual: posterior hand in renal angle, anterior hand below costal margin. Ballottable (moveable up between hands). Can get above it (vs spleen/liver). Resonant to percussion (bowel in front).

steps.title: Aorta
steps.text: — Midline pulsation above umbilicus. Expansile pulsation (width >3 fingers) = AAA. Normal aorta pulsates but does not expand laterally.

Pain WORSE on tensing = abdominal wall pain. Pain LESS on tensing = intra-abdominal

cards.title: Abdominal Percussion & Auscultation

rows: Splashing sound on shaking upper abdomen (≥3 hrs post-meal)
Renal artery stenosis, hepatocellular carcinoma, AV malformation

steps.title: Liver percussion
steps.text: — Upper border: start at 2nd ICS MCL, percuss down. Lower border: start at RIF, percuss up. Normal span 6–12 cm. Liver dullness: disappears in pneumoperitoneum (air over liver).

steps.title: Splenic percussion
steps.text: — Traube's space (left lower anterior thorax, bounded by liver, stomach, costal margin). Normally tympanic. Dull = splenomegaly or pleural effusion/consolidation.

steps.title: Bowel sounds
steps.text: — Auscultate for 30 seconds minimum. Normal = intermittent gurgles every few seconds.

cards.title: Cranial Nerve Examination

rows: Anosmia: head injury, frontal meningioma, COVID-19
Snellen chart (VA), visual fields (confrontation), colour vision, pupil reflexes, fundoscopy
Reduced VA, field defects, RAPD, optic disc swelling/pallor
EOM: H-pattern. Look for ptosis, pupil size
CN III palsy: down-and-out pupil, dilated pupil (posterior communicating artery aneurysm = SURGICAL emergency)
Head tilt, diplopia going down stairs
Light touch 3 divisions (forehead/cheek/chin), jaw clench (masseter), corneal reflex (sensory limb)
Facial numbness, loss of corneal reflex
Medial deviation of eye at rest, cannot abduct. Long intracranial course → false localising sign in raised ICP
Raise eyebrows, close eyes tight, show teeth, puff cheeks. Note nasolabial fold asymmetry.
UMN: lower face weakness only (forehead spared). LMN (Bell's): entire face including forehead = Bell's palsy
Whisper test, Rinne & Weber tuning fork tests
Conductive vs sensorineural hearing loss. Vestibular: nystagmus, Dix-Hallpike
Say "aah" — palate rises symmetrically. Gag reflex (IX = afferent, X = efferent). Hoarse voice = X

Shrug shoulders (trapezius) and turn head against resistance (SCM)
Weakness: neck dissection, posterior fossa lesion
Tongue: protrude, move side to side. Look for wasting/fasciculations.
Deviation toward side of lesion (LMN), wasting + fasciculations

pearls.text: CN III palsy with a DILATED pupil = posterior communicating artery aneurysm until proven otherwise. Emergency CT angiography. cards.title: Upper Limb Neurological Exam

pearls.label: 🟡 Pearl
pearls.text: Drift test: upward drift of one arm = cerebellar pathology. Pronator drift (arm pronates and drifts down) = contralateral UMN lesion. Pseudoathetosis (writhing movements) = proprioceptive loss. Perform this test for a full 30 seconds with eyes closed.

steps.title: Inspection
steps.text: — Wasting (thenar/hypothenar/intrinsic), fasciculations, involuntary movements, posture, drift test (outstretched arms, eyes closed — 30 seconds)

steps.title: Tone
steps.text: — Flex/extend at wrist and elbow. Increased (spasticity = UMN, clasp-knife; rigidity = cogwheel/lead-pipe = Parkinson's), decreased = LMN/cerebellar

steps.title: Power (MRC 0–5)
steps.text: — 0=none, 1=flicker, 2=gravity eliminated, 3=against gravity, 4-=slight resistance, 4=moderate resistance, 4+=strong resistance, 5=normal. Test: shoulder abduction (C5), elbow flexion (C5/6), elbow extension (C7), wrist extension (C7), finger extension (C7/8), finger flexion (C8), finger abduction (T1)

steps.title: Reflexes
steps.text: — Biceps C5/6 (musculocutaneous), Brachioradialis C5/6 (radial), Triceps C7 (radial). Grade 0 (absent) to 4+ (clonus). Compare sides.

steps.title: Sensation
steps.text: — Light touch, pin-prick (spinothalamic: pain/temp), vibration (posterior columns), proprioception. Test dermatomes C5-T1. Document level.

steps.title: Coordination
steps.text: — Finger-nose test (intention tremor = cerebellar), dysdiadochokinesia (rapid alternating movements)

cards.title: Lower Limb Neurological Exam

pearls.label: 🟡 Pearl
pearls.text: Cauda equina syndrome: saddle anaesthesia + bowel/bladder dysfunction + bilateral leg weakness/numbness = neurosurgical emergency. MRI spine within 4 hours. Missing this diagnosis = medicolegal catastrophe.

steps.title: Inspection
steps.text: — Wasting (quadriceps, calf, foot), fasciculations, pes cavus (hereditary motor neuropathy), foot drop

steps.title: Tone
steps.text: — Roll leg on bed (observe foot rotation), then passive flex/extend knee. Spasticity = legs may scissor or catch. Clonus at ankle: rapid dorsiflexion, sustained >3 beats = UMN.

steps.title: Power
steps.text: — Hip flexion (L1/2), hip extension (L5/S1), knee flexion (L5/S1), knee extension (L3/4), ankle dorsiflexion (L4/5), ankle plantarflexion (S1), big toe extension (L5)

steps.title: Reflexes
steps.text: — Knee (L3/4 femoral), ankle (S1 sciatic), plantar (Babinski): stroke lateral sole with orange stick. Upgoing = Babinski positive = UMN (or normal in infants <2yrs)

steps.title: Sensation
steps.text: — Test L1–S1 dermatomes. Saddle anaesthesia (S2/3/4) = cauda equina emergency.

steps.title: Coordination
steps.text: — Heel-shin test: slide heel from opposite knee down tibia. Ataxia = cerebellar.

cards.title: Cerebellar & Gait Examination

alerts.label: DANISH mnemonic for cerebellar signs
alerts.text: Dysdiadochokinesia · Ataxia (gait/truncal) · Nystagmus · Intention tremor · Slurred speech (dysarthria/scanning) · Hypotonia

rows: Arm flexed/adducted, leg extended, circumduction of affected leg
Both legs stiff, crossing over (scissoring), walks on toes
Bilateral UMN: cerebral palsy, cord compression, MS
Stooped, shuffling, festinating (accelerating), reduced arm swing, en-bloc turning, freezing
Wide-based, staggering, falls to side of lesion, cannot tandem walk
Wide-based, high-stepping, foot slapping, worse with eyes closed (Romberg +ve)
Posterior column disease: subacute combined degeneration, tabes dorsalis, diabetic neuropathy
Exaggerated hip/knee flexion to clear foot, foot slaps
Common peroneal nerve palsy, L4/5 root, CMT
Short stance phase on painful side
Pelvis drops to unaffected side during stance on affected leg
Weak hip abductors (gluteus medius): OA hip, hip fracture, Paget's
Magnetic gait — feet seem glued to floor, takes small shuffles, trunk/arms normal
Normal pressure hydrocephalus, frontal lobe lesion

pearls.label: 🟡 Pearl
pearls.text: Romberg's test: stand feet together, eyes open (tests cerebellar function), then close eyes. Falls with eyes closed = positive Romberg = posterior column/proprioceptive problem (NOT cerebellar — cerebellar patients fall with eyes open too).

cards.title: Mini Mental State & Cognitive Testing

alerts.label: MMSE (Folstein) — 30 points
alerts.text: Mild impairment: 21–24 · Moderate: 10–20 · Severe: <10. Adjust for education/age.

alerts.label: 4AT — Rapid delirium screening (preferred at bedside)
alerts.text: Alertness + AMT4 (age, DOB, place, year) + Attention (months backward) + Acute change: Score 0 = unlikely delirium. ≥4 = possible delirium. Takes <2 minutes. More sensitive than MMSE for delirium.

rows: Name 2 objects, repeat phrase, 3-step command, read/write sentence, copy pentagon

pearls.label: 🟡 Pearl
pearls.text: The clock drawing test is a quick screen: ask patient to draw a clock face showing 10 past 11. Tests visuospatial, executive function, and semantic memory. Any error is significant. Also assess: frontal lobe (verbal fluency — say as many animals as possible in 1 minute — <12 suggests frontal impairment).

cards.title: GALS Screen (Gait, Arms, Legs, Spine)

alerts.label: GALS is a 3-minute screening tool — if ANY abnormality found, go to detailed regional examination

pearls.label: 🟡 Pearl
pearls.text: Always ask screening questions before GALS: "Do you have any pain or stiffness in muscles, joints, or back?" "Can you dress yourself without difficulty?" "Can you walk up and down stairs?" Positive screening = detailed examination needed.

steps.title: Gait
steps.text: — Ask patient to walk to end of room and back. Normal? Any limp, asymmetry, antalgic gait, difficulty turning?

steps.title: Arms
steps.text: — "Hands behind head" (shoulder abduction/external rotation). "Hands out in front" (wrist extension). "Turn hands over" (supination). "Make a fist" (grip). Squeeze MCPJs (tenderness). Touch each finger to thumb (fine motor). Inspect hands (swelling, deformity).

steps.title: Legs
steps.text: — Patient supine. Feel for knee effusion (patellar tap, bulge sign). Passive hip flexion/internal rotation. Passive knee flexion. Squeeze MTPs (tenderness). Inspect feet (deformity, callosities).

steps.title: Spine
steps.text: — "Look at the wall, look at the floor" (cervical flex/extend). "Look over shoulder" (rotation). "Run hand down leg" (lumbar lateral flexion). Thoracolumbar extension. Schober's test if ankylosing spondylitis suspected.

cards.title: Hand & Wrist Examination

rows: Fingers deviated toward ulna at MCPJs
Finger locks in flexion, snaps with extension
Flexor tendon sheath thickening: DM, RA, idiopathic

steps.title: Inspect dorsum and palm
steps.text: — Swelling (MCPJs, PIPJs, DIPJs, wrists), deformity (ulnar deviation, swan neck, boutonnière, Z-deformity of thumb), muscle wasting (thenar, hypothenar, intrinsic), skin changes (psoriatic plaques, vasculitis, tophi)

steps.title: Palpate
steps.text: — Squeeze MCPJs (metacarpophalangeal tenderness = RA). Feel each joint individually. Temperature (warm = active synovitis). Subcutaneous nodules (extensor surfaces = RA). Tophi (helix of ear, olecranon = gout).

steps.title: Move
steps.text: — Wrist: flex/extend/deviation. Fingers: flex/extend/abduct/adduct. Opposition of thumb. Grip strength (compare sides).

steps.title: Function
steps.text: — "Do up a button / pick up a coin / write your name" — functional tests.

cards.title: Knee Examination

steps.title: Inspect standing
steps.text: — Valgus (knock-knee), varus (bow-leg), genu recurvatum, swelling, quadriceps wasting, skin changes

steps.title: Patellar tap
steps.text: — Empty suprapatellar pouch by stroking medial/lateral to patella, press patella down — tap = effusion (>30mL)

steps.title: Bulge sign
steps.text: — Milk fluid from medial compartment to lateral, then press lateral — bulge reappears medially. Small effusions (<30mL).

steps.title: Range of motion
steps.text: — Normal: 0° extension to 135° flexion. Loss of full extension = locked knee (meniscal tear/loose body), flexion contracture (OA)

steps.title: Ligament tests
steps.text: — MCL: valgus stress at 30°. LCL: varus stress at 30°. ACL: anterior draw (Lachman at 30°, most sensitive). PCL: posterior draw test.

steps.title: Meniscal tests
steps.text: — McMurray's: external rotation + extension (medial meniscus), internal rotation + extension (lateral). Click or pain = positive.

cards.title: Hip & Spine Examination

pearls.label: 🟡 Pearl
pearls.text: Schober's test <20cm at maximal forward flexion = restricted lumbar movement. Combined with morning stiffness >45min, buttock pain, improved with exercise = ankylosing spondylitis until proven otherwise. Check HLA-B27 and MRI sacroiliac joints.

steps.title: Hip inspection
steps.text: — Trendelenburg test: stand on one leg, pelvis should not drop on opposite side. Positive = weak ipsilateral hip abductors (OA, fracture, Paget's)

steps.title: Hip ROM (FABER)
steps.text: — Flexion (120°), ABduction (45°), External Rotation (45°). Thomas test: flex both hips maximally, extend one — fixed flexion deformity. FABER/FABERE pain = hip pathology or SI joint.

steps.title: Spine inspection
steps.text: — Scoliosis (Adam's forward bend test), kyphosis, lordosis, muscle spasm, skin overlying spine

steps.title: Cervical spine
steps.text: — Flex/extend (chin to chest / look up), rotation (chin to shoulder), lateral flexion. Spurling test: compress neck + rotate — radiculopathy if reproduces arm pain

steps.title: Lumbar spine
steps.text: — Schober's test (ankylosing spondylitis): mark 5cm below and 10cm above L5/S1, bend forward — should increase >5cm (total >20cm). SLR: raise straight leg — <60° + sciatic radiation = L4/5/S1 disc herniation

cards.title: Thyroid Examination

pearls.label: 🟡 Pearl
pearls.text: Pemberton's sign: raise arms above head for 1 minute — facial plethora, JVP rise, or stridor = superior vena caval obstruction from retrosternal goitre. Always check for tracheal deviation and dysphagia in large goitres.

steps.title: Inspect from front
steps.text: — Neck swelling? Visible goitre? Ask patient to swallow (sip of water) — thyroid moves up on swallowing (distinguishes from other neck masses)

steps.title: Palpate from behind
steps.text: — Both hands around neck, thumbs at back. Feel isthmus (over trachea), lobes (lateral). Size, consistency (soft = colloid goitre, firm = Hashimoto's, hard/irregular = malignancy), tenderness (thyroiditis), nodules (single vs multinodular)

steps.title: Palpate on swallowing
steps.text: — Feel movement with swallow. Does it move? (Should = thyroid/thyroglossal cyst). Fixed mass = malignancy.

steps.title: Percuss
steps.text: — Over sternum for retrosternal extension. Dull = retrosternal goitre

steps.title: Auscultate
steps.text: — Thyroid bruit (thyrotoxicosis: Graves' disease — increased vascularity)

steps.title: Eyes
steps.text: — Exophthalmos, lid lag, lid retraction, chemosis, ophthalmoplegia (Graves' ophthalmopathy)

steps.title: Other signs
steps.text: — Pretibial myxoedema (Graves'), thyroid acropachy (periosteal new bone — rare)

cards._legacyHtml:
1
Inspect from front — Neck swelling? Visible goitre? Ask patient to swallow (sip of water) — thyroid moves up on swallowing (distinguishes from other neck masses)
2
Palpate from behind — Both hands around neck, thumbs at back. Feel isthmus (over trachea), lobes (lateral). Size, consistency (soft = colloid goitre, firm = Hashimoto's, hard/irregular = malignancy), tenderness (thyroiditis), nodules (single vs multinodular)
3
Palpate on swallowing — Feel movement with swallow. Does it move? (Should = thyroid/thyroglossal cyst). Fixed mass = malignancy.
4
Percuss — Over sternum for retrosternal extension. Dull = retrosternal goitre
5
Auscultate — Thyroid bruit (thyrotoxicosis: Graves' disease — increased vascularity)
6
Eyes — Exophthalmos, lid lag, lid retraction, chemosis, ophthalmoplegia (Graves' ophthalmopathy)
7
Other signs — Pretibial myxoedema (Graves'), thyroid acropachy (periosteal new bone — rare)
🟡 Pearl
Pemberton's sign: raise arms above head for 1 minute — facial plethora, JVP rise, or stridor = superior vena caval obstruction from retrosternal goitre. Always check for tracheal deviation and dysphagia in large goitres.

cards.title: Cushing's Syndrome Examination

pearls.label: 🟡 Pearl
pearls.text: Always ask about steroid medication — iatrogenic Cushing's is far more common than endogenous. Investigate if suspected: 24-hr urine cortisol, midnight salivary cortisol, or 1mg overnight dexamethasone suppression test. Then localise: ACTH-dependent (pituitary = Cushing's disease, ectopic) vs ACTH-independent (adrenal tumour).

cards._legacyHtml:
Central obesityTruncal fat with thin limbs (buffalo hump, moon face, supraclavicular fat pads)
Purple striaeWide (>1cm), purple/red stretch marks on abdomen, thighs, breasts. White striae = normal.
Thin skin & bruisingSkin easily bruised, paper-thin, poor wound healing
HypertensionOften significant, secondary to cortisol mineralocorticoid effects
Proximal myopathyDifficulty rising from chair, climbing stairs — test: stand from seated without arms
Acne & hirsutismAndrogen excess — facial hair in women, acne
Glucose intoleranceCortisol → insulin resistance → secondary diabetes

Investigate if suspected: 24-hr urine cortisol, midnight salivary cortisol, or 1mg overnight dexamethasone suppression test. Then localise: ACTH-dependent (pituitary = Cushing's disease, ectopic) vs ACTH-independent (adrenal tumour).

cards.title: Visual Acuity & Visual Fields

rows: Optic nerve or retina (anterior to chiasm)
Optic tract, radiation, or visual cortex (posterior to chiasm) — contralateral side
Inferior temporal lobe (Meyer's loop) — contralateral superior temporal field lost
Macula, optic nerve (optic neuritis: MS, temporal arteritis)

pearls.label: 🟡 Pearl
pearls.text: Bitemporal hemianopia: the patient cannot see things to the left with the left eye OR to the right with the right eye — they bump into things on both sides. Classic presentation of pituitary macroadenoma. First test: Snellen (VA often preserved early). Always check visual fields in headache + visual symptoms.

steps.title: Snellen chart
steps.text: — 6 metres from chart, one eye covered, with glasses/contact lenses. Record as 6/6 (normal) to 6/60 (counts fingers). <6/60: count fingers, hand movements, light perception.

steps.title: Pinhole test
steps.text: — Reduced VA through pinhole = refractive error (correctable). No improvement = other pathology (cataract, macular, neural).

steps.title: Visual fields by confrontation
steps.text: — Examiner and patient at eye level, 1 metre apart, cover opposite eyes. Wiggle finger in each quadrant. Compare patient's response to examiner's (who has normal fields).

cards.title: Pupil Examination

rows: CN III palsy (surgical: posterior communicating artery aneurysm ★, herniation). Atropine drops.
Horner's syndrome: ptosis + miosis + anhidrosis (Pancoast tumour, carotid dissection, lateral medullary syndrome)
Brain death, severe hypoxia, atropine, cocaine, sympathomimetics
Optic neuritis (MS ★), optic nerve compression, severe retinal disease, amblyopia
Reacts to accommodation but NOT to light = neurosyphilis ("prostitute's pupil — accommodates but doesn't react")

steps.title: Inspect both pupils
steps.text: — Size (normal 2–6mm), shape (round, regular), symmetry. Anisocoria = unequal pupils

steps.title: Direct light reflex
steps.text: — Shine light from temporal side into one eye — that pupil constricts (direct reflex)

steps.title: Consensual light reflex
steps.text: — Light in one eye — opposite pupil also constricts

steps.title: Swinging flashlight test (RAPD)
steps.text: — Swing light rapidly between eyes. Relative afferent pupillary defect (Marcus Gunn): the affected eye's pupil DILATES when light shone on it (optic nerve problem)

steps.title: Accommodation reflex
steps.text: — Ask patient to look at distant object, then near finger — both pupils constrict

cards.title: Fundoscopy

alerts.label: Technique
alerts.text: Dilate pupils (tropicamide 1%) if possible. Dark room. Set ophthalmoscope to +2 lens initially. Approach from temporal side at 15°. Focus on optic disc first, then vessels, macula (ask patient to look directly at light).

rows: Disc swelling, blurred margins, haemorrhages, engorged veins. Absent venous pulsation earliest sign.
Raised ICP ★ (tumour, hydrocephalus, hypertensive emergency, IIH)
Dot/blot haemorrhages (deep), flame haemorrhages (superficial), microaneurysms (earliest), exudates, new vessels (proliferative)
Silver/copper wiring (AV ratio <2/3), AV nipping, flame haemorrhages, cotton-wool spots, papilloedema (grade 4)
"Stormy sunset" — flame haemorrhages in all quadrants (CRVO) or one quadrant (BRVO)
Pale retina, cherry-red spot at macula
Emboli (carotid atheroma, cardiac), giant cell arteritis
Cup:disc ratio >0.5, nasal shift of vessels

pearls.label: 🟡 Pearl
pearls.text: Papilloedema is a bilateral finding — unilateral disc swelling = usually optic neuritis or ischaemic optic neuropathy. In papilloedema, the earliest sign is loss of venous pulsation (only visible in 80% of normals). Always examine the fundus in headache, visual symptoms, hypertensive emergency, and suspected raised ICP.

cards.title: Ear Examination

rows: Stand behind patient at 60cm. Exhaled whisper of 3 numbers. Repeat other ear.
Failure to repeat = significant hearing loss
Fork on mastoid (BC) — when sound gone, move to external ear (AC)
Rinne +ve (AC > BC) = normal OR sensorineural. Rinne –ve (BC > AC) = conductive hearing loss ★
Lateralises to WORSE ear = conductive. Lateralises to BETTER ear = sensorineural on other side.

pearls.label: 🟡 Pearl
pearls.text: Rinne negative (BC > AC) + Weber lateralises to SAME ear = CONDUCTIVE hearing loss on that side (e.g. glue ear, wax, otosclerosis). Rinne positive + Weber lateralises to OPPOSITE ear = SENSORINEURAL hearing loss on Weber side (e.g. acoustic neuroma, noise damage).

steps.title: External inspection
steps.text: — Auricle shape, size, position (low-set = chromosomal syndromes), pre-auricular pits/tags, discharge, scars (mastoidectomy). Behind ear: swelling over mastoid = mastoiditis (Bezold abscess)

steps.title: Otoscopy
steps.text: — Pull pinna up and back (adults) or down and back (children). Insert speculum. Examine: canal (wax, discharge, foreign body, swelling), tympanic membrane (normal = pearl-grey, cone of light at 5 o'clock)

steps.title: Tympanic membrane
steps.text: — Perforation (central = safe, marginal = cholesteatoma risk), retraction pockets, fluid level/bubbles (OME), haemotympanum (basal skull fracture), chalk patches (tympanosclerosis)

cards.title: Nose & Throat Examination

pearls.label: 🟡 Pearl
pearls.text: A unilateral tonsil larger than the other = malignancy until proven otherwise (tonsillar lymphoma). Quinsy (peritonsillar abscess): uvula deviates away from affected side + unilateral swelling + trismus. Emergency I&D needed. Epiglottitis: tripod position + stridor + drooling + toxic — do NOT examine throat (can precipitate complete obstruction).

steps.title: Nose inspection
steps.text: — External: shape, deviation, swelling, trauma. Occlude each nostril: patent? Nasal speculum: mucosa (pale/boggy = allergic rhinitis, red = infective), septum (deviation, perforation — cocaine, Wegener's, sarcoid), turbinates, polyps

steps.title: Throat (oropharynx)
steps.text: — Adequate lighting + tongue depressor. Lips, teeth, gums, tongue (movement, atrophy, fasciculations), buccal mucosa (aphthae, white patches = leukoplakia/candida), palate (high arched = Marfan's), tonsils, posterior pharyngeal wall, uvula

steps.title: Tonsils grading
steps.text: — 1 = within tonsillar pillars. 2 = at pillar level. 3 = beyond pillars. 4 = "kissing tonsils" (touching midline)

steps.title: Voice
steps.text: — Hoarse = recurrent laryngeal nerve palsy (left: aortic arch pathology, lung/mediastinal ca; right: thyroid ca, right-sided lesions) or laryngeal pathology. Muffled "hot potato" voice = peritonsillar abscess.

cards.title: Neck Examination & Lymph Nodes

rows: Reactive lymphadenopathy (infection ★): URTI, dental, EBV, CMV
Abscess (TB cold abscess — fluctuant but NOT hot)
Intra-abdominal malignancy (gastric, pancreatic, colorectal) — via thoracic duct

pearls.label: 🟡 Pearl
pearls.text: Always examine ALL lymph node groups, not just where the patient complains. A cervical lump in an adult >40 is malignant until proven otherwise. "Never biopsy a lymph node unless you have excluded a primary source." — look for primaries in head, neck, mouth, lungs, abdomen first.

steps.title: Inspect
steps.text: — Swelling, asymmetry, scars, skin changes, pulsations (carotid), distended veins (SVC obstruction)

steps.title: Palpate lymph nodes systematically
steps.text: — Submental → submandibular → pre-auricular → post-auricular → occipital → anterior cervical → posterior cervical → supraclavicular (Virchow's) → axillary

steps.title: Lymph node character
steps.text: — Size, consistency (soft/firm/hard/rubbery), tenderness, fixation to skin or deep tissues, overlying skin changes

cards.title: Breast Examination

alerts.label: Always ensure a chaperone is present and explain each step beforehand

pearls.label: 🟡 Pearl
pearls.text: Triple assessment for breast lumps: clinical examination + imaging (USS if <35, mammogram if >35) + core needle biopsy. Do not discharge a patient with a breast lump on the basis of clinical examination alone. Paget's disease of nipple (eczematous change) = underlying DCIS or invasive cancer until proven otherwise.

steps.title: Inspect — 4 positions
steps.text: — Arms at side, hands on hips (tenses pectorals), hands behind head, lean forward. Look: symmetry, skin changes (peau d'orange, erythema, ulceration), nipple changes (inversion — new vs old, discharge, eczematous = Paget's), contour changes, visible lumps.

steps.title: Palpate (supine, arm behind head)
steps.text: — Systematic: 4 quadrants + tail of Spence (axillary tail) + areola/subareolar. Use flat of fingers in circular motion. Any lump: site, size (cm), shape, surface, consistency, mobility, tenderness, skin tethering, deep fixation.

steps.title: Nipple
steps.text: — Squeeze gently from the periphery toward nipple. Note discharge (serous/sanguineous/purulent). Single duct vs multiple duct discharge.

steps.title: Lymph nodes
steps.text: — Axillary (4 groups: anterior, posterior, medial, apical), supraclavicular, cervical. Support patient's arm with your hand to relax pectorals.

cards.title: Per Speculum & Bimanual Examination

alerts.label: ⚠️ Always ensure consent, chaperone, privacy, and patient positioning (lithotomy or left lateral) before examination

rows: Pain on moving cervix = PID, ectopic pregnancy ★
Normal variant (20%), or fixed = endometriosis, PID
Ovarian cyst/tumour, ectopic, tubo-ovarian abscess, hydrosalpinx
Cervical cancer until proven otherwise — refer under 2-week wait

steps.title: External inspection
steps.text: — Vulva: skin (erythema, leukoplakia, lesions, ulceration, warts), Bartholin's glands, urethral meatus, vaginal introitus

steps.title: Speculum examination
steps.text: — Warm speculum, insert obliquely then rotate. Open to visualise cervix. Note: discharge (colour, odour), erosion (ectropion), polyps, nabothian cysts, contact bleeding. Take smear/swabs if indicated.

steps.title: Bimanual examination
steps.text: — Two fingers (index + middle) vaginally, one hand abdominally. Assess: cervical excitation (cervical motion tenderness = PID/ectopic), uterine size/position/mobility/tenderness, adnexal masses/tenderness (ovaries)

cards.title: Male Genitourinary Examination

alerts.label: Always ensure chaperone and consent before examination

rows: Testicular cancer ★ until proven otherwise — urgent USS
Painful, high-riding testis + absent cremasteric reflex
Testicular torsion ★★★ — surgical emergency. Do NOT wait for Doppler if high suspicion.
Epididymo-orchitis (STI: chlamydia in young; UTI organism in older men)
Varicocele — left-sided ★. Right-sided = possible retroperitoneal mass (renal ca compressing IVC)
Hydrocele (surrounds testis) or epididymal cyst (separate from testis)

pearls.label: 🟡 Pearl
pearls.text: Testicular torsion: "if in doubt, take them out." Any patient with acute scrotal pain under 35 is torsion until proven otherwise. Torsion most common 12–18 years (bimodal peak in neonates). Window for testicular salvage: 6 hours (100%) → 12 hours (50%) → 24 hours (<10%). Never delay for investigations if clinical suspicion is high.

steps.title: Penis inspection
steps.text: — Phimosis (non-retractile foreskin), paraphimosis (retracted, constricting), hypospadias, epispadias, discharge (urethritis), lesions (warts, ulcers = STI, SCC)

steps.title: Scrotal inspection
steps.text: — Oedema (local vs referred), skin changes, position of testes

steps.title: Palpate each testis
steps.text: — Size (normal 4×3cm), consistency, tenderness. Lie finger and thumb along testis. Separate epididymis (posterior). Get above swelling — can you get above it? (Cannot = inguinoscrotal hernia)

steps.title: Transillumination
steps.text: — Apply light — transilluminates = fluid (hydrocele, epididymal cyst). Does NOT transilluminate = solid (hernia, tumour, haematocele)

cards.title: Skin Signs of Systemic Disease

pearls.label: 🟡 Pearl
pearls.text: ABCDE for melanoma: Asymmetry, Border (irregular), Colour (variable), Diameter (>6mm), Evolution (change over time). Any pigmented lesion that is changing, itching, or bleeding = dermatology referral. Never biopsy a suspicious melanoma in primary care — refer for excisional biopsy by specialist.

cards._legacyHtml:
Acanthosis nigricansVelvety hyperpigmentation in axillae/neck/groin — Insulin resistance ★, T2DM, GI malignancy (sudden onset in adult)
Pyoderma gangrenosumPainful ulcer with undermined purple border — IBD ★ (Crohn's/UC), RA, haematological malignancy
Dermatitis herpetiformisIntensely itchy vesicles on extensor surfaces — Coeliac disease ★ (IgA deposits in dermis)
Necrobiosis lipoidicaShiny yellow-brown plaques with telangiectasia on shins — Diabetes mellitus ★
XanthomataTuberous (over joints), tendon (Achilles/extensor tendons), xanthelasma (eyelids) — Hyperlipidaemia ★, FH
Spider naeviCentral arteriole + radiating vessels, blanch with pressure — ≥5 on trunk = liver disease ★ (portal hypertension)
Gottron's papulesPurple papules over MCPJs/PIPJs — Dermatomyositis ★ (also heliotrope rash around eyes)
Café-au-lait spotsLight brown macules — Neurofibromatosis type 1 ★ (≥6 spots >1.5cm = NF1 diagnostic criterion)
Osler-Weber-Rendu (HHT)Telangiectasia on lips/tongue/fingers — Hereditary haemorrhagic telangiectasia (recurrent epistaxis)
🟡 Pearl

cards.title: Nutritional & Fluid Assessment

pearls.label: 🟡 Pearl
pearls.text: MUST score (Malnutrition Universal Screening Tool): BMI + weight loss + acute disease effect. Use on all inpatient admissions. Malnutrition prolongs hospital stay, increases infection risk, delays wound healing, and independently predicts mortality. Always check MUST on admission and refer to dietitian if score ≥2.

steps.title: Nutritional assessment
steps.text: — BMI = weight(kg)/height²(m). Under 18.5 = underweight. 18.5–24.9 = normal. 25–29.9 = overweight. ≥30 = obese. Waist circumference: >94cm (M) / >80cm (F) = metabolic risk.

steps.title: Signs of dehydration
steps.text: — Dry mucous membranes, sunken eyes, reduced skin turgor (pinch test — slow recoil), reduced urine output, tachycardia, orthostatic hypotension, confusion (late sign)

steps.title: Signs of fluid overload
steps.text: — Peripheral pitting oedema, elevated JVP, S3 gallop, bibasal crackles, ascites, pleural effusions, pulmonary oedema on CXR

steps.title: Malnutrition signs
steps.text: — Bitot's spots (Vit A deficiency), angular cheilitis (B2, iron, folate), glossitis (B2, B3, B6, B12, iron), koilonychia (iron), peripheral neuropathy (B1/B12), pellagra skin (B3)
